nameMeanings.name: George
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Via Old French and Latin, from Greek Georgios (a derivative of geōrgos ‘farmer’, from gē ‘earth’ + ergein ‘to work’). This was the name of several early saints, including the shadowy figure who is now the patron of England (as well as of Germany and Portugal). If the saint existed at all, he was perhaps martyred in Palestine in the persecutions of Christians instigated by the Emperor Diocletian at the beginning of the 4th century. The popular legend in which the hero slays a dragon is a medieval Italian invention. He was for a long time a more important saint in the Orthodox Church than in the West, and the name was not much used in England during the Middle Ages, even after St George came to be regarded as the patron of England in the 14th century. Its use increased from the 1400s, and by 1500 it was regularly among the most popular male names. This popularity was reinforced when George I came to the throne in 1714 , bringing this name with him from Germany. It has been one of the most popular English boys' names ever since.

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Sir George Monbourcher was born in 1304, in Gamston, Nottinghamshire, England, his father, Bertram Monboucher II, was 29 and his mother, Joan Charron, was 10. He married Margarett Chaucer from 9 January 1328 to 8 January 1329, in Nottinghamshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 1 son and 4 daughters.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Sir George Monbourcher est né en 1304, à Gamston, Nottinghamshire, Angleterre, son père, Bertram Monboucher II, avait 29 ans et sa mère, Joan Charron, avait 10 ans. Il a épousé Margarett Chaucer du 9 janvier 1328 au 8 janvier 1329, à Nottinghamshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 1 fils et 4 filles.

de.lifeSummary: Als Sir George Monbourcher 1304 in Gamston, Nottinghamshire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Bertram Monboucher II 29 und seine Mutter Joan Charron 10. Er hat von 9. Januar 1328 bis 8. Januar 1329 in Nottinghamshire, England, United Kingdom Margarett Chaucer geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 1 Sohn und 4 Töchter.

it.lifeSummary: Quando Sir George Monbourcher è nato il nel 1304 nel Gamston, Nottinghamshire, England, il suo padre, Bertram Monboucher II, aveva 29 anni e il suo madre, Joan Charron, aveva 10 anni. Lui sposato Margarett Chaucer da 9 gennaio 1328 a 8 gennaio 1329, nel Nottinghamshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 1 figlio e 4 figlie.

ko.lifeSummary: Sir George Monbourcher(이)가 1304년 영국 노틴그함실르 갬스턴에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Bertram Monboucher II(은)는 29세였고, 그의 어머니 Joan Charron(은)는 10세였다. 그는 1328년 1월 9일부터 1329년 1월 8일까지 영국 잉글랜드 노틴그함실르에서 Margarett Chaucer(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 한 명의 아들과 네 명의 딸을 두었다.
